nodes.name: NT
nodes.summary: NT表示 IUCN 近危等级，当前关联914种鸟类，如帝企鹅、小斑几维鸟、大美洲鸵等914种鸟类。

nodes.name: 鹳形目
nodes.summary: 鹳形目（Ciconiiformes）是图谱中的目级分类节点，当前关联17种鸟类，如钳嘴鹳、非洲秃鹳、Greater adjutant等17种鸟类。

nodes.name: 白颈鹳
nodes.summary: 白颈鹳（Asian woolly-necked stork，学名 Ciconia episcopus），分类上属于鹳形目，并归入鹳科，在图谱中标记为近危（NT）物种，主要分布于法里達巴德、哈里亚纳邦，常见栖息地包括峡谷峭壁、湿地、稀树草原等4项，主要威胁包括森林砍伐。
nodes.shortSummary: 白颈鹳（学名：Ciconia episcopus；英語：woolly-necked stork，whitenecked stork）属鹳形目鹳科鹳属，广泛分布于亚洲和非洲大部分地区。2011年，中国鸟类学家在云南省纳帕海自然保护区也发现了白颈鹳物种。 外形特征 绝大多数白颈鹳的…

nodes.name: 鸟纲
nodes.summary: 鸟纲（Aves）是图谱中的分类级分类节点，当前关联10767种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、朱鹮、中华秋沙鸭等10767种鸟类。

nodes.name: 鹳属
nodes.summary: 鹳属（Ciconia）是图谱中的分类级分类节点，当前关联8种鸟类，如黑鹳、Maguari stork、东方白鹳等8种鸟类。

nodes.name: 法里達巴德
nodes.summary: 法里達巴德是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括白颈鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 鹳科
nodes.summary: 鹳科（Ciconiidae）是图谱中的科级分类节点，当前关联17种鸟类，如钳嘴鹳、非洲秃鹳、Greater adjutant等17种鸟类。

nodes.name: 峡谷峭壁
nodes.summary: 大型猛禽和食腐鸟偏好的高崖栖息环境。

nodes.name: 哈里亚纳邦
nodes.summary: 哈里亚纳邦是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括白颈鹳，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 沼泽
nodes.summary: 具有较高含水量和密生植被的浅水环境。

nodes.name: 森林砍伐
nodes.summary: 原生林被砍伐导致栖息地面积和连通性下降。

nodes.name: 白颈鹳
nodes.summary: 白颈鹳（Ciconia episcopus）是图谱中的分类级分类节点，当前关联1种鸟类，如白颈鹳。

nodes.name: 湿地
nodes.summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

nodes.name: 动物界
nodes.summary: 动物界（Animalia）是图谱中的分类级分类节点，当前关联10767种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、朱鹮、中华秋沙鸭等10767种鸟类。

nodes.name: 脊索动物门
nodes.summary: 脊索动物门（Chordata）是图谱中的分类级分类节点，当前关联10767种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、朱鹮、中华秋沙鸭等10767种鸟类。

nodes.name: 稀树草原
nodes.summary: 东非常见的开放草地与湿地边缘复合景观。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: In the fields near Hodal in [[Faridabad District of Haryana, India|thumb|left]] It is a widespread tropical species which breeds in Asia, from India to Indonesia. It is a resident breeder building nests on trees located on agricultural fields or wetlands, on natural cliffs, and on cell phone towers.{{Cite journal|last1=Rahmani|first1=A

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: Gopi|date=2020|title=Comparing abundance and habitat use of Woolly-necked Storks Ciconia episcopus inside and outside protected areas in Myanmar|url=https://storkibisspoonbill.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/2020Win_Final.pdf|journal=SIS Conservation|volume=2|pages=96–103|eissn=2710-1142}} They are attracted to fires in grasslands and crop fields where they capture insects trying to escape the fire. They use ponds and marshes inside forests in Asia, especially in south-east Asia where they use grassy and marshy areas in clearings in several forest types. In India, they are an uncommon species in coastal habitats. They use coastal areas in Asia with birds in Sulawesi observed to be eating sea snakes. In an agricultural landscape in north India, woolly-necked storks preferred fallow fields during the summer and monsoon seasons, and natural freshwater wetlands during the winter. Here, irrigation canals were preferentially used during winters when water levels were low, and birds avoided crop fields in all seasons

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: Gopi|date=2020|title=Comparing abundance and habitat use of Woolly-necked Storks Ciconia episcopus inside and outside protected areas in Myanmar|url=https://storkibisspoonbill.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/2020Win_Final.pdf|journal=SIS Conservation|volume=2|pages=96–103|eissn=2710-1142}} They are attracted to fires in grasslands and crop fields where they capture insects trying to escape the fire. They use ponds and marshes inside forests in Asia, especially in south-east Asia where they use grassy and marshy areas in clearings in several forest types. In India, they are an uncommon species in coastal habitats. They use coastal areas in Asia with birds in Sulawesi observed to be eating sea snakes. In an agricultural landscape in north India, woolly-necked storks preferred fallow fields during the summer and monsoon seasons, and natural freshwater wetlands during the winter. Here, irrigation canals were preferentially used during winters when water levels were low, and birds avoided crop fields in all seasons

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: The species was elevated from "Near-threatened" to "Vulnerable" in 2014 based on anecdotal reports of deforestation in south-east Asia potentially leading to catastrophic population declines with the assumption that the species required protected wetlands inside forested reserves. It was, however, down listed to "Near-threatened" in 2019 after concrete evidence emerged from south Asia and Myanmar that the erstwhile population estimate was a severe underestimate, and that most woolly-necked storks used agricultural areas and unprotected wetlands, with abundances being lower inside forested reserves. Agricultural landscapes in north India support considerable numbers of breeding pairs that have relatively large brood sizes and behaviors similar to storks in protected managed reserves suggesting that this species is not an obligate wetland bird and that it is not reliant on undisturbed protected wetlands and forest reserves. An earlier "guesstimate" of the south and south-east Asian population of woolly-necked storks of 25,000 has been revised upwards to an estimated > 2,00,000 storks in south Asia alone. Counts carried out along the Mekong river in Cambodia (first survey in 2006 and 2007, followed by another in 2018) showed some variations in numbers of Woolly-necked Storks counted.{{Cite journal |last1=Mittermeier |first1=J
